narrative_blocks.header: He grew up inside his mother’s seminary
narrative_blocks.body: John Hart Willard was eleven when his parents, Dr. John and Emma Willard, brought the female seminary to Troy in 1821 at the invitation of the city’s leading citizens. He had been born in Middlebury, Vermont, in 1810, the only child of his father’s marriage to Emma.

narrative_blocks.header: Schooled by the best, then West Point
narrative_blocks.body: His early education came from some of the country’s finest instructors, including the father of Bishop Huntington at Hadley, Mr. Adams of Bennington, and Capt. Partridge of Middletown. He completed his studies at the West Point military academy, which he entered in 1826.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried the school into a second generation
narrative_blocks.body: When Emma Willard remarried and stepped back in 1838, John Hart Willard and his wife took charge of the Troy Female Seminary. He remained one of the city’s best-known citizens until his death in 1883 at seventy-three.

narrative_blocks.header: He grew up inside his mother’s seminary
narrative_blocks.body: John Hart Willard was eleven when his parents, Dr. John and Emma Willard, brought the female seminary to Troy in 1821 at the invitation of the city’s leading citizens. He had been born in Middlebury, Vermont, in 1810, the only child of his father’s marriage to Emma.

narrative_blocks.header: Schooled by the best, then West Point
narrative_blocks.body: His early education came from some of the country’s finest instructors, including the father of Bishop Huntington at Hadley, Mr. Adams of Bennington, and Capt. Partridge of Middletown. He completed his studies at the West Point military academy, which he entered in 1826.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried the school into a second generation
narrative_blocks.body: When Emma Willard remarried and stepped back in 1838, John Hart Willard and his wife took charge of the Troy Female Seminary. He remained one of the city’s best-known citizens until his death in 1883 at seventy-three.

military_service.role: Ship’s crew, then quartermaster, Connecticut regiment
military_service.notes: Captured by the British and later released

narrative_blocks.header: Captured at sea, he came home to heal
narrative_blocks.body: John Willard served on a ship’s crew at the outbreak of the Revolution, was captured by the British, and after his release became quartermaster of a Connecticut regiment. Only after the war did he train as a physician and open a practice in Middlebury, Vermont.

narrative_blocks.header: Jefferson made him a federal marshal
narrative_blocks.body: A committed Democratic-Republican, Willard backed Thomas Jefferson in 1800 and was rewarded with appointment as United States Marshal for Vermont, an office he held from 1801 to 1811. He succeeded Jabez G. Fitch and was followed by David Robinson.

narrative_blocks.header: A stolen bank key nearly ruined him
narrative_blocks.body: As a director of the Vermont State Bank, Willard was held liable when $28,000 vanished in 1812, drawing liens against his property. The money had been taken with a duplicate key; though the thief was eventually exposed and the liens lifted, his finances never recovered and he died insolvent.

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the business behind a famous school
narrative_blocks.body: When the bank scandal threatened the family, his third wife Emma opened a boarding school for young women, and John became its business manager. Together they moved the seminary from Middlebury to Waterford and finally to Troy, New York, in 1822.

narrative_blocks.header: Three wives across one eventful life
narrative_blocks.body: Willard first married Esther Wilcox, who died in 1788, then Mindwell Meigs, widow of Thaddeus Frisbie. In 1809 he married Emma Hart of Middlebury, the educator whose school would carry the Willard name into history.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew judged New York from the bench
narrative_blocks.body: Willard’s nephew and namesake, John Willard (1792–1862), became a prominent New York jurist, serving as a justice of the state Supreme Court and briefly on the Court of Appeals. The two Willards, uncle and nephew, both rose from Guilford, Connecticut, to public prominence in the North.

education.degree: Medical training under a local physician (apprenticeship)

sources.short: Amherst College Archives — Emma Hart Willard Papers

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the first women's higher-education school
Won her divorce against a controlling second husband
Cousin Elihu Burritt read her address
Corresponded with cousin James Gates Percival
Educated twelve relatives at Troy at her expense
Teaching at 17, running an academy at 19

quotes.text: Words cannot tell of the agonies I have endured. He found my fame and my own opinion of possessing talent above the common mind inconvenient to him, and he set himself systematically to work to bend and break my spirit.
quotes.attribution: Emma Willard, letter to a close friend, describing her marriage to Dr. Christopher C. Yates, 1839

quotes.text: We are distant relations, your mother having been of the same name and stock as my father... I knew and loved your father — he was our family physician.
quotes.attribution: Emma Willard, letter to James Gates Percival, March 19, 1832 — Yale Beinecke, YCAL MSS 703

institutions.institution_blurb: Founder and head of Troy Female Seminary 1821-1838.

landmarks.notes: Built 1809; her Middlebury home and first school site

education.degree: Studied then taught; ran the academy by 1806

Revolutionary Soldiers of the Town of Berlin (Berlin-Peck Memorial Library)

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded at Saratoga, September 1777
narrative_blocks.body: Serving as a lieutenant in General Wolcott's detachment, Samuel Hart was wounded at Saratoga on September 19, 1777, and two years later led the Berlin company that turned out at the New Haven alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: A thinker who could not speak unwritten
narrative_blocks.body: A great reader, he could not address an audience without first writing out his ideas; in religion he turned Universalist after a church dispute.

notable.primary_url_label: Berlin in the French and Indian War (p. 13)
notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary captain, father of Emma Hart Willard

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both commanded Connecticut militia in the Revolution.

documents.document_blurb: Emma Willard's 1855 poem names both parents — the most direct portrait of Samuel Hart's character.
Almira Hart Phelps article on the Hart family, NYGB Record.

military_service.unit: Col. Cook's Regiment; Berlin militia company
military_service.notes: Lieutenant under Gen. Wolcott's detachment at Saratoga, wounded 19 Sep 1777; captain of the Berlin company at the New Haven alarm, 1779, repelling Tryon's invasion.
Fifteen days, 8-22 Aug 1757, in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry.

career.role: First Clerk and Treasurer, Berlin Congregational Church
career.notes: Withdrew from the Second Congregational Church in 1807 after a religious dispute.

documents.document_blurb: Emma Willard's 1855 poem names their parents Samuel Hart and Lydia Hinsdale.

narrative_blocks.header: A Massachusetts town was named for her brother
narrative_blocks.body: Her brother Rev. Theodore Hinsdale was the first Congregational minister of a new settlement in Berkshire County, Massachusetts — first settled in 1763 and incorporated in 1804 as the town of Hinsdale, named in his honor. He and Lydia were children of Capt. John Hinsdale of Kensington.

burialCemetery.name: Oakwood Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Burial place of John Paine Cushman (X01916), Maria Jones Tallmadge Cushman (HD1033), Harriet Delafield Cushman Balch (HD2816), and Rev. John Paine Cushman Jr. (HD2817).
